nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs at depths of 3-70 m, typically 5-20 m; non-migratory and reef-associated.

diet.notes: In the wild, feeds on polychaete worms, coral polyps, crustaceans and mollusc eggs. In captivity, generally requires a varied diet of meaty fare: frozen mysis, finely chopped shrimp/clam/mussel on the half shell, brine shrimp, and small crustaceans. Newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods initially and may need live mysis or fresh seafood to be coaxed into eating. Feed multiple times daily.

avoidWith: ornamental shrimp and crabs (will be eaten)
stony and soft corals (will nip polyps)
very aggressive triggers or large damsels (will stress the butterflyfish)
conspecifics outside of an established mated pair

summary: A peaceful Western Atlantic butterflyfish marked with two broad black body bars and a third bar through the dorsal fin. Generally not reef-safe and tends to be a finicky feeder on arrival; best for a mature, fish-only or FOWLR aquarium.
careNotes:
The Banded Butterflyfish is a Western Atlantic/Caribbean species that has been reported from Massachusetts south to Santa Catarina (Brazil), including the Gulf of Mexico and Saint Paul's Rocks. It is generally considered moderately demanding: a mature display with stable water chemistry, plenty of live rock providing crevices and caves, and ample open swimming space tend to give the best results.

Cross-source spread on minimum tank size is wide — LiveAquaria and Marinebio recommend roughly 120-125 US gallons (≈450-475 L), while reefapp.net lists 300 L. Because adults reach around 16 cm, are active mid-water swimmers, and are often kept as a bonded pair, the larger value is used here (≈450 L / ~150 cm length).

Temperature recommendations also differ: LiveAquaria states 72-78°F (≈22-26°C, conservative aquarium-trade range), while FishBase wild temperature data is 25-28°C (mean 27.2°C). An averaged 23-27°C is used here, which spans both.

Generally not reef-safe: has been reported to feed on tubeworms, feather dusters, clams (including Tridacna), and to nip at LPS, SPS, and soft coral polyps. Best housed in a fish-only or FOWLR system. Multiple specimens can sometimes be kept together if introduced simultaneously, but established pairs should not be split.

Juveniles show a distinct color phase featuring a large ringed black ocellus on the rear of the body in addition to the body bars, which fades as the fish matures. Like many butterflyfishes, the species is described as forming monogamous pairs in the wild.

Feeding can be problematic on arrival: newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods and may need live mysis, fresh clam/mussel on the half shell, or other meaty fresh seafood to be coaxed into eating; multiple feedings per day are generally advised, especially during acclimation.
breedingNotes: Oviparous, pelagic spawner; forms monogamous pairs during breeding. Has not been reliably bred in home aquaria; almost all specimens in the trade are wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of aquarium husbandry parameters (temperature, pH, dKH, specific gravity, minimum tank size, care level, diet category, reef-safe status).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range, depth, maximum length (16.0 cm TL), wild temperature range (25-28°C, mean 27.2°C), wild diet, and reproductive mode (oviparous, monogamous pair-bonding).

additional.name: MarineBio
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (~125 gal / ≈473 L) and confirmed unsuitability for reef tanks; corroborated feeding difficulty on arrival in captivity.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Additional cross-check on adult size (16 cm), temperament (peaceful), reef compatibility (not reef-safe; nips Tridacna), and the difficulty of getting newly added specimens to start feeding.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawners forming monogamous pairs; not known to have been bred in home aquaria.
